questions.question: What must you download first to start using Notion Calendar?

options.explanation: The Notion desktop app is for general Notion work, not the separate calendar application.
Notion Calendar is a standalone app that you need to download before you can use its calendar features.
Google Calendar is a third‑party service that can be synced, but it isn’t the app you download.
Apple Calendar is also a separate service; you still need the Notion Calendar app.

questions.question: Which keyboard shortcut opens the Share availability window in Notion Calendar?

options.explanation: T jumps back to today, not to the share window.
Pressing S opens the Share availability window so you can mark free time.
N moves to the next upcoming event, not to sharing availability.
? opens the list of all shortcuts, but does not directly open the share window.

questions.question: When a database entry lacks a start and end time, how does Notion Calendar display it?

options.text: All‑day events at the top of the day view
options.explanation: Items without specific times appear as all‑day events at the top of the calendar.
Recurrence is a separate setting; missing times do not make an event recurring.
Busy blocks are created by event blocking, not by missing times.
The entry still shows on the calendar; it isn’t just a reminder.

questions.question: How does the conflict‑avoidance feature prevent double‑booking when sharing your availability?

options.text: It hides all busy events from the calendar view
options.explanation: Busy events remain visible; they are just excluded from the shareable time slots.

options.text: It only shows time slots that don’t overlap any busy events
options.explanation: When enabled, the share link offers only slots that are free of busy events.

options.text: It automatically cancels any meeting that would overlap a new booking
options.explanation: The feature does not cancel meetings; it simply prevents overlapping slots from being offered.

options.text: It merges all calendars into a single calendar to avoid conflicts
options.explanation: Merging isn’t how conflict avoidance works; it filters slots based on existing busy events.

questions.question: When creating a new event and you want to attach an existing Notion page, which option should you select?

options.explanation: The Docs and links button lets you link an existing Notion page or create a new one for the event.
Participants adds invitees, not Notion pages.
Conferencing link adds a Zoom/Google Meet link, not a Notion page.
Location specifies a physical or virtual place, not a Notion document.

questions.question: Which calendar view is recommended for an overview of monthly product launches?

options.explanation: Weekly view shows only one week at a time, not the full month.
Daily view is too granular for a month‑long overview.
Switching to monthly view gives a broad view of all launches scheduled for the month.
Timeline view isn’t mentioned in the guide for product roadmaps.

questions.question: What effect does enabling event blocking for your personal calendar have on your work calendar?

options.text: Personal events are hidden from the work calendar
options.explanation: Blocking doesn’t hide events; it marks them as busy.

options.text: “Busy” blocks appear on the work calendar for personal events
options.explanation: Event blocking creates busy blocks on the work calendar so personal events protect work time.

options.text: Personal events are deleted from the personal calendar
options.explanation: Blocking never deletes events; it only reflects them as busy on another calendar.

options.text: Only personal events are shown in a separate tab
options.explanation: Blocking doesn’t create a new tab; it adds busy blocks to the existing view.

questions.question: Where does Notion Calendar display the countdown timer for an upcoming event?

options.explanation: The card shows event details but not the persistent countdown timer.
The countdown appears in the menu bar, giving a constant reminder.
The sidebar lists calendars and settings, not the countdown.
While notifications exist, the guide specifies the menu bar for the countdown.

questions.question: If a Notion database item includes a start and end time, what happens when you drag its event to a new time slot on the calendar?

options.text: Only the calendar view updates; the database stays unchanged
options.explanation: The integration is bidirectional; changes sync back to the database.

options.text: The Notion database entry updates automatically to reflect the new time
options.explanation: Dragging updates the underlying database entry so both views stay in sync.

options.text: A duplicate event is created in the calendar
options.explanation: Dragging moves the existing event; it does not duplicate it.

options.text: Nothing changes; the event is locked to its original time
options.explanation: Events can be moved; the guide describes drag‑and‑drop functionality.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT considered a “busy” event for conflict avoidance?

options.text: A focus time or out‑of‑office event
options.explanation: Focus time/out‑of‑office events are listed as busy.

options.text: An accepted meeting (yes) or tentative meeting (maybe)
options.explanation: Accepted or tentative meetings count as busy.

options.text: An event you created or organized
options.explanation: Events you create are also treated as busy.

options.text: A reminder without a specific time
options.explanation: Reminders without a time are not counted as busy events for conflict avoidance.
